exemplar.title: How do differing concentrations of nitric acid, affect the mass of Buccinum Undatum shells (whelk shells)? School Lab, June 2024

detailedExemplarData.title: How do differing concentrations of nitric acid, affect the mass of Buccinum Undatum shells (whelk shells)? School Lab, June 2024

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

strengths: Well-structured with clear headings, sub-sections and logical flow.
weaknesses: Occasional typographical errors and inconsistent citation formatting.
Lapses in concision and minor coherence issues due to missing figure labels.
Some verbosity and spacing inconsistencies affect clarity.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report is very well structured and uses appropriate terminology, fulfilling top-band structure criteria. However, typographical errors, inconsistency in citations, lapses in concision and minor coherence issues prevent full marks. Overall coherence is maintained but not flawless, aligning with band 2, for 2/3.

strengths: Focused, coherent research question that clearly defines IV, DV and context.
Detailed environmental issue discussion with real-world examples.
Clear explanation linking acid deposition to shell mass loss.
weaknesses: Lacks broader policy context and global scale discussion.
Does not address time-scale differences between lab exposure and natural acid rain.
Connections could integrate food-web effects more explicitly.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The research question is succinct, testable and focused, fully meeting top-band descriptors. The environmental context (acid rain chemistry, real-world examples) is strong, though it omits broader policy and global impact discussion. The links between acid deposition and shell mass loss are well explained chemically and ecologically, with only minor omissions (e.g., lab versus natural timescales). Overall performance aligns with the 5â6 band but slight depth omissions justify 5/6.

strengths: Detailed step-wise, repeatable method with clear n=3 replicates.
Sampling strategy described with a range of acid concentrations.
Risks and ethical considerations tabulated with basic mitigations.
weaknesses: No standardization of shell particle size and missing pH measurement steps.
Weighing method flawed (test-tube rack mass masks small shell changes).
Risk assessment omits inhalation hazards and eyewash station references.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The method is largely repeatable and produces relevant data, fitting the 3â4 band, but key omissions (particle size control, pH verification, balance protocol) prevent full sufficiency. The sampling strategy is described but insufficiently justified in choice of intervals and replicates. The risk assessment outlines main hazards and mitigations but lacks comprehensive coverage (e.g., inhalation risks, eyewash stations). Performance sits mid-band, earning 3/6.

strengths: Identifies key strengths such as precise acid concentration control.
Acknowledges methodological weaknesses like particle size variation.
Relates conclusion significance to the environmental context.
weaknesses: Improvement suggestions are general and lack methodological detail.
Does not quantify impact of identified limitations.
Lacks comprehensive discussion of method limitations and confidence.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The discussion identifies some strengths and weaknesses and links conclusions to environmental implications, but suggestions for improvement are superficial and lack specificity. There is no quantitative evaluation of limitations or detailed methodological critique. Performance aligns with the 1â2 band, earning 2/6.

strengths: Comprehensive raw data tables with means, standard deviations and error bars.
Effective scatter graph displaying all relevant data.
Conclusions are valid and directly supported by observed trends.
weaknesses: Missing units in some table headings and inconsistent figure numbering.
Inconsistency in Spearmanâs rank calculation and no formal significance tests.
No RÂ² value reported and lack of quantitative graph discussion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation is well organized with tables, error bars and graphs, placing it at the top of the 3â4 band. Analysis identifies patterns but has calculation inconsistencies (Spearmanâs rank) and omits significance testing and RÂ². The conclusion correctly interprets trends and is supported by data, but could be strengthened with quantitative measures. Overall, performance merits 4/6.

strengths: Proposes catalytic converters with clear mechanism-based justification.
Evaluates cost disadvantages relative to emission reduction.
Links the solution directly to findings on acid deposition.
weaknesses: Evaluation omits socio-economic feasibility in developing regions.
Does not consider alternative policy measures or technology constraints.
Lacks thorough assessment of catalytic converter efficiency limits.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The application of catalytic converters is described and justified based on study findings, and some strengths and weaknesses are evaluated. However, broader feasibility issues and alternative solutions are not explored. Performance fits the mid-band descriptor, thus 2/3.

strandSelections.reasoning:
Scientific terms (acid deposition, mol dmâ»Â³, Spearmanâs rank) are used appropriately.  However, typographical errors (âaddictedâ for âaffectedâ, spacing in units) and occasional verbosity reduce concision.

Because only one of the two qualities (terminology vs. concision) is fully met, band 2 (score 2) is selected.

The evaluation revisits how higher acidity erodes calcium carbonate and briefly links this to ecosystem impacts and management, but does not critically weigh alternative explanations or discuss confidence intervals.  The environmental significance is only superficially explored.

Accordingly, it âdescribes how some aspects of the conclusion relate to the environmental issueâ, fitting the 1-2 band; a full 2 marks are awarded.

The student states that increasing acid concentration increases mass loss, cites specific mean values, links to the hypothesis and rejects the null hypothesis.  The trend is compared to error bars to argue reliability, and secondary literature is used for support.

Because the conclusion is valid and explicitly supported by presented data, the strand satisfies the top of the 3-4 band (score 4).

Means, standard deviations and Spearmanâs rank are calculated, and patterns are described.  However, the Spearman table contains internal inconsistencies (all d = 0 yet râ recalculated as 0.956) and the student mis-states the 1 mol SD comment.  No rate calculations or statistical tests of significance (t-test) are given.

Thus the data are analysed correctly in part but incompletely, matching the 3-4 band; a mark of 3 recognises the partial errors.

The student proposes two extensions (test other calciferous materials; field studies) and mentions more focused conservation, but no detailed methodological improvements (e.g., sieving particle sizes, using pH meter continuously) are given.  Suggestions remain fairly general.

These are âsuperficial modifications and further researchâ, meriting 1 mark in the 1-2 band.

A step-wise method, specific volumes, masses, timing and repetitions (n = 3) are supplied, making the investigation broadly repeatable.  Data produced demonstrate that the procedure can generate results that address the RQ.

Nonetheless, several weaknesses remain: shell particle size is not standardised, pH confirmation is omitted, and weighing the test-tube plus rack introduces unnecessary mass that masks small changes.  These limitations prevent classification as fully âappropriate and sufficientâ; hence the top of the 3-4 band (4) is most appropriate.

Links between acid deposition and calcium-carbonate shell erosion are explained chemically and ecologically, and the student clarifies why whelk shells model calciferous habitats.  The narrative explicitly ties nitric-acid concentration to anticipated mass loss and hence to biodiversity loss.

Although convincing, the explanation could reference time-scale differences between lab exposure and natural rainfall more explicitly.  The coverage is still comprehensive, justifying a high scoreâ5 within the 5-6 band.

The research question is expressed succinctly (âHow do differing concentrations of nitric acid affect the mass of Buccinum undatum shells?â), identifies both IV and DV, specifies the organism, and sets clear experimental limits.  It is neither overly broad nor ambiguous, allowing direct data collection within a school laboratory.

The wording stays consistent throughout the report and is directly testable with the method provided.  It therefore meets the descriptor of being relevant, coherent, and focused, justifying placement in the top band.

Advantages (source reduction) and a key disadvantage (high cost) are discussed, but the evaluation omits feasibility in developing countries, catalytic efficiency limits, or alternative policies.  Thus âsome relevant strengths and weaknessesâ are evaluated, aligning with band 2 (score 2).

Headings, sub-headings and step numbering produce a coherent report; tables and figures are logically ordered.  No sections appear out of place and the flow is easy to follow.

This fulfils the descriptor of being well structured and organised throughout, earning the maximum 3 marks.

Strengths (precise acid concentrations, balance accuracy) and one weakness (uneven particle size) are identified.  Limitations such as temperature control, surface area measurement, and weighing method are omitted, and no discussion of their quantitative impact is provided.

This meets the descriptor of âidentifies some strengths and weaknessesâ, earning 2 marks within the 1-2 band.

The student devotes over a page to explaining acid deposition, the chemistry of SOâ/NOâ formation, typical pH values, and multiple realâworld examples (Chiltern Hills, Everglades, Adirondack lakes).  These illustrations make the environmental issue clear and relevant.

Minor depth is missing on the scale of global impacts and policy context, so the discussion, while strong, is not exhaustive; a score of 5 (rather than 6) best captures the quality within the top band.

The student describes choosing six concentrations from 1 mol dmâ»Â³ to 0 mol dmâ»Â³ and labels this a âstratified sampleâ, explaining that higher concentrations model long-term exposure.  While this constitutes a description of the strategy, justification for the exact concentration intervals and the number of replicates is limited.

Because the reasoning is present but not fully justified, the work aligns with "describes the choice of sampling strategy" (band 3-4).  A mid-band mark of 3 is warranted.

A tabulated risk assessment considers chemical burns, broken glass and slips, assigning probability, magnitude and mitigation.  Ethical disposal of acids is discussed.  The coverage is clear but not exhaustive (e.g., no mention of eye-wash stations, corrosive fume inhalation, or environmental disposal regulations).

This satisfies an outline of risks and ethics, fitting the 3-4 band; a score of 3 reflects the partial, but not comprehensive, treatment.

All raw data are presented in organised tables with headings, units and means; processed data include SD and a scatter graph with error bars.  Minor issues exist (units absent in some column headings, figure numbering inconsistencies), but the essential information is conveyed accurately.

Because diagrams for all relevant data are present and largely correct with only minor omissions, the top of the 3-4 band (4) is appropriate.

A single solutionâwidespread adoption of catalytic convertersâis outlined with justification based on reducing NOâ/SOâ at source.  The explanation links directly to acid rain formation, providing more than a mere statement.

This clear description with basic justification corresponds to band 2 (score 2).

The argument generally proceeds logically from introduction to conclusion, but lapses occur (e.g., inconsistent râ values, missing figure labels) that momentarily hinder clarity.  Overall coherence is maintained, yet not flawless.

This meets âmainly logical but difficult to follow in partsâ, placing it in band 2 with 2 marks.

annotations.comment: The research question is focused, identifying both IV and DV clearly, specifying organism and experimental context, enabling testability and coherence with the method.
Solid explanation of acid rain chemistry linking SOâ/NOâ emissions to pH changes; consider expanding to include policy measures affecting emissions for broader context.
Discussing real-world acidification in Adirondack lakes enriches environmental context; the inclusion of specific ecological impacts strengthens linkage to research.
Controlled variables description omits how particle size variation will be standardized; include sieving or size measurement steps to ensure reproducibility.
Hypothesis statements are clear but the alternative hypothesis has grammatical errors and could be more precise in stating predicted quantitative trends.
Risk assessment overlooks inhalation hazards of acid vapors and the need for eyewash stations; extend table to cover these to meet comprehensive safety planning.
Ethical disposal mentions technician neutralization but should reference institutional or environmental regulations to demonstrate thorough ethical planning.
Including a pH probe in equipment is good, but the method omits pH measurement steps; integrate pH checks to confirm solution concentration.
Weighing test tubes with rack introduces systematic error; recommend zeroing balance after placing empty tube or removing rack to isolate shell mass.
Qualitative description is useful, but include quantitative observations or photographs with scales to support narrative and improve data presentation.
Raw data tables are comprehensive but inconsistent figure numbering may confuse readers; ensure table captions and numbers are uniform.
Processed data include SD but lack units and clear headings for clarity; add 'mass loss (g)' and 'SD (g)' labels for precision.
Standard deviation formula is correct but variable definitions (X, Î¼, n) are not explained; include a brief key to enhance reader understanding.
Combining all change-in-mass data into Figure 7 table is effective for overview; this clear synthesis supports pattern identification.
Spearmanâs rank table shows all d=0 yet râ is recalculated as 0.956; recheck rank assignments or formula application to resolve this inconsistency.
Interpretation of statistical significance is well linked to critical values; referencing confidence levels here effectively supports conclusion validity.
Graph discussion notes linearity but would benefit from reporting RÂ² value to quantitatively confirm strength of linear relationship.
Acknowledges precise acid concentration control as a strength; linking this directly to data reliability demonstrates good experimental evaluation.
Limitation acknowledges particle size variation but does not quantify its impact; suggest evaluating surface area or conducting size-distribution analysis to strengthen critique.
Proposed extension to other calciferous materials and field studies adds ecological validity but lacks specific methodological details; outline sampling or measurement changes.
Application of catalytic converters is justified by emission reduction mechanism, demonstrating practical linking of findings to mitigation strategies.
Cost disadvantage is noted effectively but discussion omits socioeconomic or infrastructural feasibility in developing regions; include broader evaluation of this solution.
Bibliography demonstrates breadth of sources but lacks consistent citation format (e.g., missing access dates for some items); standardize references for clarity.
Title effectively frames study focus on calciferous ecosystems and acid deposition; clearly communicates research scope.
Mention of biomagnification extends impact discussion; consider linking this explicitly to potential food-web effects in calciferous aquatic ecosystems.
